title: Amino Acid Sequence
abstract:
Abstract
               Knowing the amino acid composition of a protein hydrolysate, even with high accuracy, is just like having a sackful of letters of the alphabet, all mixed up. Knowing the arrangement of these amino acids along a polypeptide chain—or, sometimes, several connected polypeptide chains —is another matter altogether: words and sentences are now formed. This experimental transition was first achieved by Cambridge biochemist Frederick Sanger (1918–1.) during a period lasting more than ten years, from about 1945 to 1955.
